Both use refractive index mu_r: Snell law gives refraction geometry while Brewster gives the polarisation angle from the same mu_r.
mu_r links critical angle (total internal reflection) with Brewster angle (polarisation), both derived from the same medium.
Angle of refraction theta in Snell law and diffraction angle theta in single-slit minima can be equated in problems involving a diffracting prism.
Mass m links thermal energy Q = mc*DeltaT with gravitational weight W = mg in calorimetry problems involving falling or suspended bodies.
Mass m links latent heat Q = mL with F = ma in problems where a phase-changing substance also undergoes mechanical change.
Length L links thermal expansion DeltaL = L*alpha*DeltaT with strain DeltaL/L; thermal strain combines both equations.
de Broglie wavelength lambda from lambda = h/p feeds into wave speed v = f*lambda for matter waves in a medium.
Rydberg gives 1/lambda of emitted photon; wave number k = 2*pi/lambda directly uses that wavelength.
de Broglie lambda = h/p uses the same classical momentum p = mv; a photon-electron collision problem chains both equations.
Kmax from Einstein photoelectric equation equals the classical kinetic energy K = 0.5*m*v^2 for the ejected electron.
Binding energy BE = DeltaM*c^2 is a direct application of mass-energy equivalence E = mc^2.
Q value of a nuclear reaction uses the same DeltaM*c^2 formula as mass-energy equivalence.
Both follow the same exponential decay form N = N0*e^(-lambda*t) and DeltaT = DeltaT0*e^(-k*t); useful for comparing decay constants.
Work W done by gas in first law Q = DeltaU + W is the same mechanical work W as in work_energy_power; bridges thermal and mechanical analysis.
Isothermal work done W = nRT*ln(Vf/Vi) is the W in the work-energy theorem when all input energy is mechanical.
Pressure P in ideal gas PV = nRT is the absolute pressure defined as P = F/A; problems on gas in containers chain both.
Pressure P in adiabatic PV^gamma = const is the same thermodynamic pressure as in Bernoulli theorem for compressible flow analysis.
Heat Q in first law is the same Q as in Q = mc*DeltaT; calorimetry problems span both equations to find final temperature and work done.
Heat Q absorbed by the engine maps directly to calorimetric Q = C*DeltaT for a gas heated from a reservoir.
density equation supplies mass m directly into F=ma for problems where material density and volume are given instead of mass
mass from density equation feeds into weight W=mg
mass from density feeds into KE = 0.5*m*v^2 for energy problems
mass from density feeds into p=mv for momentum problems
